The MSCI World Index is a measure of the combined performance of large and medium sized companies from developed stock markets around the world. It provides a useful comparison against which the Fund's performance can be assessed over time.

The Investment Association (IA) groups funds with similar geographic and/or investment remit into sectors. The Fund's ranking within the sector (as calculated by a number of data providers) can be a useful performance comparison against other funds with similar aims.

Investment policy

The Fund invests at least 70% of its assets in shares (also known as equities) of companies, of any size, in any industry and in any country, provided that such companies are not excluded by virtue of the applicable exclusions and meet the Fund’s sustainability standard. Whether a company provides sustainable products and/or services, and therefore whether it is a sustainable company for this Fund, will be determined by a robust evidence-based standard, which requires the majority of each company’s current revenues (at least 50%) to be aligned to a single sustainability theme. The Fund may also invest up to 30% of its assets in other assets including: (a) companies that have less than 50% revenues aligned to the sustainability themes or have revenues which the Investment Manager considers as enhancing quality of life and which are not considered to conflict with the sustainability themes: (b) companies that derive at least 50% of prospective revenues from products and services that contribute to positive environmental or social change; (c) Collective Investment Schemes (including those managed by Janus Henderson); and (d) cash. The Investment Manager may use derivatives (complex financial instruments) to reduce risk or to manage the Fund more efficiently. The Fund is actively managed with reference to the MSCI World Index. The index represents the most appropriate and useful comparator for assessing the Fund's performance because there is a high degree of overlap between the constituents of the index and the initial investible universe of the Fund, but the index does not define the Fund’s investment universe. The Investment Manager has discretion to choose investments for the Fund that are not in the index or with weightings that are different compared to the index. As an additional means of assessing the performance of the Fund, the IA Global sector average, which is based on a peer group of broadly similar funds, may also provide a useful comparator.

Investment strategy

The Fund pursues its objectives by investing in sustainable companies that the Investment Manager believes contribute to positive environmental or social change and which have attractive financial prospects based on detailed analysis of financial statements and forecasted cash flows. The Fund has a bias towards “growth” equities. The “growth” approach emphasises investments in companies the Investment Manager believes have the potential for above-average earnings growth. Examples of themes the Investment Manager has identified include efficiency, cleaner energy, water management, environmental services, sustainable transport, sustainable property & finance, safety, quality of life, knowledge & technology and health. More product-specific information, including our investment principles, can be found in the Document Library of the website at www.janushenderson.com. Further information as to how Janus Henderson approach ESG, including Janus Henderson’s “ESG Investment Policy”, can be found at www.janushenderson.com/esg-governance.

Fund-specific risks

Shares/Units can lose value rapidly, and typically involve higher risks than bonds or money market instruments. The value of your investment may fall as a result. Shares of small and mid-size companies can be more volatile than shares of larger companies, and at times it may be difficult to value or to sell shares at desired times and prices, increasing the risk of losses. The Fund follows a growth investment style that creates a bias towards certain types of companies. This may result in the Fund significantly underperforming or outperforming the wider market. Due to the Fund’s Sustainability Approach, it may exclude or invest less or more in certain companies and may therefore perform differently to funds that have a similar financial objective, but that do not have a binding Sustainability Approach. Political, regulatory or investor sentiment about sustainability initiatives may change over time and impact the Fund’s performance. The Fund may use derivatives with the aim of reducing risk or managing the portfolio more efficiently. However this introduces other risks, in particular, that a derivative counterparty may not meet its contractual obligations. If the Fund holds assets in currencies other than the base currency of the Fund, or you invest in a share/unit class of a different currency to the Fund (unless hedged, i.e. mitigated by taking an offsetting position in a related security), the value of your investment may be impacted by changes in exchange rates. When the Fund, or a share/unit class, seeks to mitigate exchange rate movements of a currency relative to the base currency (hedge), the hedging strategy itself may positively or negatively impact the value of the Fund due to differences in short-term interest rates between the currencies. Securities within the Fund could become hard to value or to sell at a desired time and price, especially in extreme market conditions when asset prices may be falling, increasing the risk of investment losses. The Fund could lose money if a counterparty with which the Fund trades becomes unwilling or unable to meet its obligations, or as a result of failure or delay in operational processes or the failure of a third party provider.

FOR MORE INFORMATION, PLEASE VISIT JANUSHENDERSON.COM

data.title: Banco Macro: Why 2026 Matters More Than Any Quarter In 2025
data.description:
It's been quite a wild ride for Argentine equities as a whole, and the banking sector is no exception.

Banco Macro S.A.'s (BMA) ADR is currently down 15% year to date, at the moment I write this, even though it has rallied by more than 100% since the victory of Milei's party in the midterm congressional elections. Those guaranteed Milei's governability and the hope for progress on the anti-inflation and fiscal-discipline agenda.

But taking a step back, the BMA's still-weak result since the beginning of the year has been driven mostly by a confluence of macroeconomic factors rather than company-specific ones.

We can list for example some factors such as (1) global emerging markets flow turned strongly against Argentina in 2025, after strong appreciation in 2024, a natural profit-taking since the "Milei trade" at the end of 2023; and the (2) rise in Argentine sovereign risk (CDS) due to exchange rate uncertainty and residual political noise post-reforms with congressional elections (mid-term) bringing extra volatility.

More specifically about Banco Macro, it's important to note that this financial institution is basically a positive anomaly in the Argentine banking system, which makes it structurally different from Grupo Galicia (GGAL), BBVA Argentina (BBAR), Santander Río (SAN), and Banco Nación (state-owned).

Banco Macro dominates the inland provinces of Argentina (outside Buenos Aires), present in regional economies and agribusiness, which has historically given it higher spreads, more captive clients, and low competition.

In Argentina, Banco Macro is very strong in mass retail and micro/SME, with a low average ticket and simple products, which contrasts with Galicia and BBVA, with more diversified and corporate orientations. It has the lowest leverage ratio in the system and has the highest excess capital in Argentina by far (see graph below for H1 '25). This makes Macro the bank best positioned to consolidate the sector.

But understanding Banco Macro's competitive advantages only makes sense when we look at the bigger picture for Argentina in 2025.

In an Argentine macroeconomic scenario of a stressed exchange rate and falling inflation, what do banks need to do to ensure that the structural thesis doesn't break down?

In my view, and potentially that of the market, the correct answer would be something like keeping the structural pillars intact, even if the quarter's P&L comes in ugly.

After all, when inflation falls too quickly (1) the result per monetary position falls; (2) the NIM falls; (3) the gain on bonds falls; (4) the effect of indexations diminishes; (5) the quarterly ROE becomes a mess.

And when the exchange rate shoots up at the same time, the bank usually increases (1) provisions; (2) the cost of hedging; (3) the perception of risk, and (4) the P&L becomes even more compressed.

This shows that there's nothing pretty about a monetary transition in Argentina, where even excellent financial institutions will report bad numbers, and in scenarios of extreme noise, the market tends not to care so much about quarterly profits and margins, but rather structural pillars.

And that's basically what Banco Macro reported in its most recent quarter in 3Q25, anchored on (1) stable or growing deposits—solid funding; (2) excess capital to protect against volatility; (3) reasonably controlled NPLs, no collapse; (4) ample liquidity in ARS and USD and high cash; (5) no destructive regulatory changes, linked to the political risk of a more statist government— not the case today.

For these reasons, the bullish structural thesis on Banco Macro, and other Argentine Banks continues, and the re-rating remains anchored at 2026 with the ADR rising even with negative earnings.

A Cold Look at Banco Macro’s Tough 3Q25

Roughly speaking and coldly interpreting the figures, the most recent results released by Banco Macro showed a weak quarter, deteriorating in terms of net interest income, results per monetary position, provisions, and also costs. That's why we're going to go through all the grotesque figures in Banco Macro's latest balance sheet.

First of all, there is a clear pressure on the bank's core earnings. NIM fell from 23.5% to 18.7% YoY, the main negative driver for the quarter. Higher non-interest-bearing deposit requirements have put pressure on spreads, and a lower contribution from inflation (which previously inflated NIM) has caused core earnings to shrink. And all of this is the result of hypersensitivity to the Argentine monetary regime.

In the deposits line, there was an increase of 5% QoQ and 11% YoY, while loans rose 3% QoQ and 69% YoY—driven by the USD currency. But since there was a sharp drop in NIM, balance sheet growth did not translate into profit. This shows Banco Macro growing in funding and portfolio, but with compressed profitability.

There was also a noticeable deterioration in credit quality, with NPLs rising from 2.1% to 3.2% (+113 bps) YoY. This deterioration requires higher provisions and directly impacts the operating result. And it hits retail and SME credit risk the hardest, a point that could be hit further down the line if the recovery in 2026 is slightly slower than expected.

The inflation-adjustment results and net income from financial assets fell 84% QoQ and 86% YoY, highlighting a strongly negative monetary position result. Basically, the inflation-driven mechanics that used to deliver automatic gains from inflation and government bonds stopped helping the bank's bottom line (and even started hindering it).

Administrative expenses rose 20% YoY, despite the closure of 21 branches, which seems strange, but shows a consequence of wage pressures indexed to inflation. In terms of profitability, annualized ROE for 3Q25 closed at -2.6%, and for the last nine months at 4.5%, at least showing structural earning power, only totally suppressed by the macro environment with falling inflation and compressed spreads.

On the more positive side, the leverage of 4.3x assets to equity ratio is lower than domestic peers, and denotes an extremely conservative balance sheet, opening up two alternatives in the medium term (2025-26): (1) acquisitions (as the market speculates); and (2) extraordinary dividends and/or buybacks. Exposure to government bonds (SP/TP) was also moderately reduced to 22.3% of assets, showing a clear sign of reduced dependence on the sovereign.

Why the Structural Thesis Remains Intact

Even with the seemingly poor results, I anticipate that there are reasons to remain constructive on Banco Macro's thesis (including an immediate bullish reaction post 3Q25).

The rationale behind this seemingly disconnected optimism with the reality of recent figures is that the market is simply not buying the quarter or nine months of 2025, but rather 2026. And nothing in Q3 '25 breaks the structural thesis that next year is likely to bring.

I would put forward two phases to better explain how Argentine banks can start making money again when the economy stabilizes. The first is the transition from high inflation to falling inflation, and the second is stabilized low inflation and a normal banking cycle.

As the graph below shows, when Javier Milei took over the presidency of Argentina, inflation was 211% per year (December 2023), it fell to 118% in December 2024, and the trend is for it to continue falling to 28% in December 2025 and 14% in December 2026. First of all, this is fantastic because it effectively shows Argentina coming out of one of the worst inflationary regimes on the planet, which will lead to an improvement in people's real lives very quickly.

With inflation at ~1.5% per month (as analysts predict in 2026), three fundamental things happen at the same time: (1) the real NIM recomposes itself, as with stable inflation the Central Bank reduces the use of extraordinary borrowing, the spread between funding costs and assets normalizes, and most importantly banks can price credit using positive real interest rates; (2) the monetary position ceases to be negative, where the hyper-inflationary accounting effect disappears; and (3) credit is reborn, real personal loans flourish, leasing, mortgages, and so Argentine banks stop living off inflationary arbitrage and go back to living off real credit. In this specific case, this is where Banco Macro, which is strong precisely in retail + SME, tends to explode in profitability.

In other words, in 2026, the mere prospect that inflation will no longer fall at explosive speed, but will converge to a healthy level, is exactly the moment that will transform banks' P&L.

Valuation: Where the Alpha Still Lies

That said, as the market reacts based on expectations, aren't these expectations of stabilizing inflation already priced in?

At the moment, Banco Macro is trading at 149% of its book value, with the theory already embedded that (1) Milei is still in power and therefore inflation will continue to fall; (2) the worst of the operational disinflation is over; and (3) 2026 will be better than 2025.

But that's only the obvious part of "stabilization," and it doesn't exhaust the possible re-rating.

I would say that the alpha of the thesis is still guided by the quality and depth of economic stabilization in 2026, and signs that go beyond disinflation to strong and sustainable stabilization leave room for a re-rating, especially since Argentina is still a country with volatile political risk (although it fell after the mid-term elections) and the exchange rate regime has not yet been completely defined. So here, I see room for a re-rating in terms of normalization rather than merely disinflation.

Another point is the return on real credit, which is the biggest driver of ROE. A strong expansion of personal credit and the return of long-term SME credit are very powerful for Banco Macro. I doubt that once the bank starts posting better and better ROE figures, it won't set a precedent for a bullish reaction. If today's consensus can see ROE going to ~12% or ~13% by 2026, if inflation stabilizes at 14% YoY and 1.5% MoM, NIM returns, and credit revives, for every one percentage point of additional ROE, fair P/B rises.

Also, I would put the use of Banco Macro's excess capital (the largest in Argentina) as a competitive advantage in this case, added to a low leverage (assets to equity ratio), yielding M&As, buybacks, and capital reduction that today do not enter the 1.4x book value that the bank trades.

Risks and Final Remarks

Investing in Argentine equities is complex, involving high exposure to political risks and a constant anchor to falling inflation. But on the other hand, they are in an economy that is undergoing one of the fastest and most profound stabilization processes in its modern history.

Banco Macro is particularly well placed in this transition, as (1) it carries the largest excess capital in the Argentine banking system; (2) it has one of the strongest retail and SME franchises outside the Buenos Aires region; (3) maintains very low leverage; and (4) retains meaningful optionality for accretive capital deployment.

In this case, if inflation does indeed stabilize at low double digits in 2026, I anticipate that the banking sector will transition from survival mode to a normal banking cycle, and nothing can be more bullish than that, as Banco Macro's earnings power re-emerges almost mechanically.

So, considering that the BMA is not a call for the next quarter, but for the next regime, and the consensus model disinflation but arguably not normalization, I think the gap is where the upside lies.

data.title: Hormel Foods: Recent Dividend Raise, Yield At Decade Low - Bargain Or Value Trap?
data.description:
Introduction

In 2025, I've grown more cautious about certain sectors as inflation continues to place downward pressure on quality businesses.

Although many have continued to perform well, one particular consumer staple, Hormel Foods (HRL) continues to face challenges, as evidenced by the decade-low share price.

With staying power and a brand name, the Dividend King could be either a great bargain at the current price, or a potential value trap. But this depends on your time horizon and portfolio strategy.

In this article, I discuss Hormel's latest earnings, upcoming quarter, and why, despite the decade-low valuation and attractive yield, I'm rating the stock a hold.

Brief Overview

Most of us are likely familiar with the food giant, Hormel Foods. The Dividend King has a long operating history, having been founded in 1981. Their founder, George Hormel, had success with the sale of the company's products, opening its first branch in 1901.

Today the company is known for their brands like Jennie-O, Skippy, SPAM, Planters, and Hormel Chili. HRL operates in three segments: Retail, Foodservice, and International.

Disappointing Investment

As previously mentioned, Hormel has been a disappointing investment over the past year. At the time of writing, the consumer staple is down a sizable 26.53%.

When you compare them to peers like The Campbell's Company (CPB) and The J.M. Smucker Company (SJM), all three food stocks have underperformed in the past year. This can be attributed to a number of factors, but higher-for-longer inflation has likely been the main contributor.

But even when you look out over a 10-year period, HRL has been a disappointing investment, down nearly 37%. As a result, shares now trade near a 10-year low, as previously mentioned. Since hitting a 52-week low of $21.03 last month, the stock has seen some price appreciation, up roughly $2 to $23.23 at the time of writing.

Mixed Q3

For income-focused investors, HRL could be a potential bargain if they can successfully turn things around. During their Q3 earnings back in August, the company saw its share price slip as a result of disappointing earnings.

EPS amounted to $0.35, missing analysts' estimates by $0.06. This was flat from the previous two quarters, while sales were $3.03 billion, beating by $50 million. While sales managed to see slight growth of 1% year-over-year, their bottom line declined from $0.37 the previous year's quarter.

Through the first 3 quarters, sales were $8.92 billion, up from $8.78 billion, while EPS was down 9.5% from $1.16 to $1.05. HRL's earnings decline was attributed to lower cash flows & income due to higher inventory and an elevated commodity market.

Comparably, peer The Campbell's Company saw 6% sales growth for fiscal year 2025. But their bottom line declined 4% from $3.08 to $2.97.

Narrowed Guidance

Another factor for the low valuation is HRL's lingering headwinds forced management to lower its full-year guidance. While they do expect a slight increase in sales, operating income and earnings per share were revised lower.

At midpoint, EPS is expected to see an 8.9% decline from the $1.58 they saw in 2024. Operating income last year amounted to $1.1 billion. So, the company is expecting a slight decline.

As a result, margins have also experienced pressure. Last fiscal year, profit margins were 17%. In Q3, these were 16.7%. Management attributed the pullback to inflation as well as softness in the food market.

I think some of this can be attributed to the explosive popularity of GLP-1 drugs, which has forced food companies like Hormel to pivot.

To combat this, the company stated they look for brands like Jennie-O, Planters, and value business to increase profit margins going forward.

Upcoming Quarter

On a positive note, HRL is expecting to see some top & bottom line growth in the upcoming quarter next week. Sales are expected in a range of $3.15 billion to $3.25 billion, while earnings are expected to increase $0.03 on the low end.

So, for shareholders, this is positive. I expect the company to see continued pressure with earnings likely flat. Especially, since management stated they expect a $0.01 to $0.02 impact from tariffs. But if the company does manage to meet or exceed, I believe the share price will likely pop.

Dividend & Balance Sheet

Probably the most attractive metric about Hormel is the dividend yield of 5%. The giant raised their dividend earlier this week by 0.9% to $0.2925. While this is significantly lower than the current rate of inflation and is a testament to the company's current headwinds, this extends the Dividend King track record.

HRL has 97 years of uninterrupted dividends and has increased the dividend for 60 years. For Q3, HRL saw free cash flow of $85 million and paid out $159 million in dividends. Through 3 quarters, free cash flow was $303.3 million and $474 million in dividends.

So, despite current pressures, I think the dividend will remain safe, at least for the near term. In 2024, HRL ended the year with a free cash flow payout ratio of 59%.

The company's balance sheet also gives them flexibility with a low leverage ratio within management's targeted range of 1.5x - 2x. Total debt was $2.9 billion, relatively small in comparison to their market cap of $12.78 billion.

Bargain Or Value Trap?

At the current price, Hormel's price is appealing at a forward P/E of 16.1x. As you can see, the stock has fallen below its intrinsic value and normal P/E of nearly 20x. Using their normal multiple, the stock offers solid upside with a price target of roughly $32 over the next 2 years.

The question is, can the company return to growth in 2026? Next year Hormel Foods expects to see 7% bottom-line growth and 9% growth in 2027 according to FAST Graphs.

During Q3, management stated they expect headwinds to carry over into 2026. And although base rates have come down and are likely to provide tailwinds, I don't think HRL will start seeing positive impacts until the back half of the year at the earliest. So, the current earnings growth expectations could be a stretch.

In short, investors shouldn't expect much upside from the current price. But you do collect a 5% dividend yield while you wait. So, from an income and valuation standpoint, Hormel could be a good fit inside an income-focused portfolio.

Moreover, the economy seems to be weakening, as evidenced by the recent uptick in unemployment. If we do see further economic weakening, I expect profit margins to continue seeing downward pressure.

Although the current yield and valuation are attractive, I would like to see economic stability, margin expansion, cash flow growth, and how management executes on expected growth strategies before considering upgrading the stock to a buy.

Bottom Line

Hormel Foods will likely continue to see downward pressure at least for the next 2 to 3 quarters.

Although interest rates are likely to be lower in the next year or two, other factors like GLP-1 drugs, higher input costs, and tariffs are likely to linger, limiting any significant upside over the near to medium-term.

Although the company could be a solid long-term play with upside, economic uncertainty and margin pressures keep me at a hold for now.
